title: Energy saving in a student or shared house
description: How to cut energy bills in a student or shared house: sorting the billing, agreeing a heating deal, what each housemate's habits cost, and renter-safe fixes.
blurb: Split incentives make shared houses expensive. How to sort the billing, agree heating and cut each housemate's costs.
lede: Shared houses should be cheap to run. One boiler, one fridge, one set of standing charges split four or five ways. In practice they are often the most expensive homes per head in the country, because nobody quite owns the bill. The person whose name is on the account is not the one taking 25-minute electric showers, the landlord who could insulate the loft never sees the bill at all, and the coldest bedroom quietly acquires a 2 kW fan heater that costs more per evening than the central heating would. The fix is less about gadgets and more about three agreements: who pays, how the house is heated, and which habits get shared instead of duplicated.
short_answer: A typical shared house can take a few hundred pounds a year off its combined bill with three moves: agree one heating schedule instead of letting plug-in heaters multiply, get everyone's name on the energy account and submit real meter readings, and share laundry and cooking loads rather than running them per person. The most expensive solo habits are long electric showers at around 41p per 10 minutes and fan heaters at about £2.09 per four-hour evening, at the 26.11p per kWh July to September 2026 cap rate.

sections.html:
Energy economists call it the split-incentive problem, and a student house is the textbook case. The landlord owns the building fabric but never pays the bills, so there is no commercial reason for them to add insulation or replace a tired boiler. The tenants pay the bills but cannot touch the fabric. And within the house, the cost of any one person's habit is split between everyone, so each housemate pays only a fifth of what their own shower, heater or gaming rig actually costs. Everything anyone wastes is 80 per cent subsidised by the others.

That structure explains most of what goes wrong. It is why the thermostat war happens, why one person's tropical bedroom is everyone's problem, and why the front door gets left on the latch in January. It also tells you where the fixes are: make costs visible, agree the shared decisions once, and concentrate on changes that need no landlord permission. Our guide to saving energy when renting covers the tenant-versus-landlord side in more detail; this page deals with the housemate-versus-housemate side.

There is one genuine structural advantage worth keeping in mind. Standing charges are billed per meter, not per person. At 57.19p a day for electricity and 29.04p for gas, that is roughly £315 a year before anyone uses a single unit. In a five-person house that fixed cost is £63 each, versus the full £315 for someone living alone. Shared living starts ahead; the job is not to throw the advantage away on duplicated habits.

sections.h2: Sort the billing before the behaviour
sections.html:
No amount of shorter showers helps if the account itself is set up badly. Four things to check in the first week of a tenancy:

  • Get every name on the account. Energy debt on a shared account is joint and several, meaning the supplier can chase any named person for the whole amount. If only one housemate is named, they carry all the risk while everyone else has none of the incentive. All names on, and agree a payment split in writing, even if it is just a group chat message.
  • Submit real meter readings, or get a smart meter. Suppliers estimate usage for houses that never send readings, and estimates for a five-person household are often badly wrong in both directions. A wrong-way estimate means either a nasty catch-up bill at the end of the tenancy, exactly when deposits are in dispute, or months of overpaying. A monthly reading takes two minutes; see how to submit meter readings. Tenants who pay the supplier directly are entitled to request a smart meter, and the in-home display is the single best tool for making one housemate's habit visible to the rest.
  • Check whether you are on a prepayment meter. Older student lets often have them left over from previous tenants. Since rates were levelised the unit prices are close to direct debit, but top-up runs and the risk of self-disconnection in a busy house make them a poor fit for sharers. Switching a prepayment meter to credit billing is usually possible if you pay the supplier directly and pass a credit check.
  • If bills are included in the rent, know the rules. Landlords reselling energy to tenants are bound by Ofgem's maximum resale price rules: they cannot charge you more than they paid, including a fair share of the standing charge. Bills-included deals usually carry a fair-usage cap, so read it, because the excess rate above the cap is set by the contract, not by the price cap.

Finally, tenants who pay the supplier directly have the right to switch supplier, whatever the tenancy agreement implies. Some agreements ask you to tell the landlord or to return the account to the original supplier when you leave, which is reasonable, but they cannot stop you switching while you live there.

sections.h2: Heating: the one decision that outweighs everything else
sections.html:
Heating is where shared houses lose the most, and it nearly always fails the same way. The house cannot agree a thermostat setting, so the central heating runs low or erratically, and the cold housemates quietly buy plug-in heaters for their rooms. That is the worst possible outcome, because electric resistance heat costs 26.11p per kWh while gas costs 7.33p. Even after boiler losses, heat from the gas central heating costs roughly 8p per kWh of warmth, about a third of the price of the same warmth from a fan heater. A 2 kW heater running four evening hours costs about £2.09 a night, around £63 a month, and that is one bedroom.

So make the heating a house decision, made once, in October:

  • Agree a schedule, not a free-for-all. Two blocks usually work for a student house: a morning hour and an evening block matching when most people are in. Set the thermostat between 18 and 20 degrees and leave it. Every 1 degree lower is worth roughly £90 a year for a typical home on Energy Saving Trust estimates, and more in a large, draughty HMO.
  • Do not leave it on low all day because someone heard it is cheaper. It is not, for almost every house, and it is one of the most persistent energy-saving myths. A house that is empty from 9 to 5 should have cold radiators from 9 to 5.
  • Turn radiators down, not off, in empty rooms. Valve setting 1 or 2 in an unused room saves money, but fully off in a poorly insulated HMO invites condensation and mould on cold external walls, which becomes a deposit problem later.
  • Give the cold housemate a cheaper option. An electric blanket runs at well under 3p an hour, against roughly 52p an hour for a fan heater. If one person genuinely runs cold, a £30 electric blanket pays for itself within weeks of displacing a heater habit.

sections.h2: What each housemate's habits actually cost
sections.html:
Costs that feel invisible when split five ways look different written down per person. These are the habits that dominate shared-house electricity bills:

HabitEnergy usedCost per useCost over time
10-minute electric shower (9.5 kW)1.6 kWh41p£151 a year, daily
Fan heater, 2 kW, four-hour evening8 kWh£2.09About £63 a month
Gaming PC, 400 W, four hours1.6 kWh42p£152 a year, daily
Condenser tumble dryer load2.5 kWh65p£68 a year, twice a week
Washing machine, 30 degree cycle0.5 kWh13p£14 a year, twice a week
Full 1.7 litre kettle from cold0.2 kWh5pOne mug costs about 1p

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Appliance figures are typical ratings; yours will vary.

Two things stand out. First, showers and heaters dwarf everything else. Five people taking daily 10-minute electric showers is over £750 a year between them, and trimming each shower to seven minutes saves the house around £225 of that. Electric shower cost breaks this down per minute. Second, the gadgets people argue about, kettles and phone chargers and the TV on standby, are mostly noise. Settle the shower and heater questions first and let the kettle go.

Laundry is the easiest genuine win because it shares so well. Five separate half loads cost five times the electricity of the same clothes washed together. A shared laundry rota, full loads at 30 degrees, and an airer instead of the tumble dryer for anything that is not urgent will quietly save a house £100 or more a year.

sections.h2: Cheap fixes nobody's landlord will mind
sections.html:
Shared houses are usually older and draughtier than average, and most of the worthwhile fixes cost a few pounds, need no permission, and can move out with you. Split the cost between housemates and it gets even cheaper. Our quick wins under a tenner page covers the full list; the ones that matter most in a shared house are:

  • Draught-proof the front door. It is the most-used door in the building and usually the leakiest. Self-adhesive foam strip around the frame, a brush strip on the letterbox and a £5 draught excluder along the bottom deal with the worst of it.
  • Swap the most-used bulbs to LED. Halls, landings, kitchen and the living room are lit for hours in a busy house. If the fittings still have halogen or incandescent bulbs, the LED swap repays itself within months, and the bulbs are yours to take when you leave.
  • Reflector panels behind radiators on external walls. Cheap foil panels push heat back into the room instead of into the brickwork, and old solid-wall student houses are exactly where they earn their keep.
  • Close the curtains at dusk, every room. Free, and worth a surprising amount through single-glazed sashes. Thicker charity-shop curtains in the coldest bedroom beat a fan heater on cost by a mile.
  • Cook together a few nights a week. One oven session feeding four people uses a quarter of the energy of four separate sessions, and one big pan beats four small ones on the hob for the same reason.

Do these as one shared job in October rather than hoping someone gets round to it. The winter energy checklist makes a decent agenda for a single house meeting before the cold sets in.

sections.h2: The bottom line for your house share
sections.html: Shared houses do not have an energy problem so much as an ownership problem. Solve that and the kilowatt-hours follow. Get every name on the account and real readings going to the supplier, so the bill is accurate and the risk is shared. Make heating one agreed schedule at 18 to 20 degrees and replace any bedroom fan heater with central heating plus an electric blanket. Share laundry and cooking instead of duplicating them, and spend one afternoon and about £20 between you on draught-proofing and LEDs. None of it needs the landlord, and together it is plausibly £200 to £400 a year off a typical shared-house bill, which is real money split four ways and even better as one less argument in the group chat.

faq.q: Who is legally responsible for the energy bill in a shared house?
faq.a: Whoever is named on the account with the supplier. If several housemates are named, liability is joint and several, meaning the supplier can pursue any one of them for the full debt, not just their share. If bills are included in the rent, the landlord holds the account and is responsible for paying the supplier. The safest setup for sharers is everyone named on the account with an agreed split in writing.

faq.q: Are bills-included tenancies a good deal for students?
faq.a: They are convenient and predictable, which has real value, but they remove any reward for using less and almost always include a fair-usage cap with a charge for going over it. Landlords reselling energy cannot legally charge more than they paid under Ofgem's maximum resale price rules. Read the fair-usage clause before signing and compare the bills-included premium with what the house would plausibly pay on its own account.

faq.q: Can tenants in a shared house switch energy supplier?
faq.a: Yes, if the tenants pay the supplier directly, they have the right to choose the supplier regardless of what the tenancy agreement prefers. Some agreements ask you to notify the landlord or to put the account back with the original supplier at the end of the tenancy, which is allowed, but a landlord cannot prevent a switch while you are paying the bills. If the landlord pays the supplier and recharges you, the choice is theirs.

faq.q: Do students get any discounts or help with energy bills?
faq.a: There is no student-specific energy discount in Great Britain. Full-time students are exempt from council tax, but energy is billed at normal rates. Support schemes such as the Warm Home Discount are tied to benefits and supplier criteria rather than student status, so most student households will not qualify. Check the current eligibility rules on the official GOV.UK and Ofgem pages, as schemes and amounts change each year.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to leave the heating on low all day in a shared house?
faq.a: Almost never. A house heats up faster than the myth suggests and loses heat the whole time it is held warm, so heating an empty house from 9 to 5 simply pays for warmth nobody feels. The cheaper pattern for a shared house is a timed schedule matching when people are actually home, with the thermostat set once between 18 and 20 degrees and left alone.
